Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I tried to block out time each day for my 16 courses, but with that many subjects, I was constantly jumping between materials. I'd usually start with good intentions, making lists and schedules, but by midweek everything would fall apart. I spent around 19 hours a week studying, but it never felt like enough given how much content I was juggling.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due. I'd have these intense bursts right before deadlines where I'd barely sleep, then crash afterward and struggle to get back on track. Regular study times were something I aspired to but rarely achieved. The anxiety of having so many courses made it hard to stick to any consistent schedule because something was always urgent.
Q3: Usually panic about a deadline or suddenly remembering I had something due. I'd also log in when I got email notifications about new assignments or announcements. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, which happened a lot given my terrible attendance rate. The fear of falling behind was a constant motivator, even if it wasn't healthy.
Q4: For a few weeks mid-semester, I got into a rhythm of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It felt almost automatic, like checking social media. But then I had a particularly rough week with multiple deadlines colliding, and that habit just evaporated. After that, it became more reactive than routine—I'd only go in when something forced me to.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were my lifeline since I learn best through reading and writing. I really appreciated when professors posted detailed notes or transcripts because I could download them and annotate at my own pace. Discussion forums were also helpful when other students asked questions I was too anxious to ask myself. Those 16 posts I made were carefully thought out because writing helped me process the material.
Q6: I barely touched the video content even though I watched some—over 200 minutes apparently. Videos felt inefficient for me because I couldn't skim or search for specific information like I could with text. I also ignored a lot of optional practice quizzes because I was so focused on just completing the required work. With 16 courses, I had to triage constantly.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam. I needed to see the immediate payoff because I simply didn't have bandwidth for anything that felt supplementary. If something helped me understand a concept I knew would be tested, I'd engage deeply. Otherwise, it went to the bottom of an ever-growing to-do list.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required work already had me stretched thin with only 66% of assignments completed. When I saw something labeled 'optional,' my brain immediately categorized it as 'skip' even if it might have been helpful. There was a constant guilt about this, but survival mode doesn't leave room for enrichment.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, print them out, and cover them in handwritten notes. I had notebooks full of summaries and concept maps that never showed up anywhere digital. I also used external resources like YouTube explainers and free online textbooks when the course materials weren't clicking for me.
Q10: There were entire weekends where I'd be deep in my notes, rewriting concepts by hand and testing myself with flashcards I made, but my LMS would show zero activity. My best learning happened away from screens, honestly. I'd take materials to the library or a coffee shop and work through them without any internet connection. That's when I actually retained information.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a webpage, look at it once online, then spend hours with the offline version. My laptop had folders full of course materials organized by week that I'd return to repeatedly. The LMS would only show that initial download, not the five times I re-read something while highlighting and taking notes.
Q12: Around week six, everything crashed for me. I had exams in multiple courses, assignments piling up, and my anxiety spiked to the point where I couldn't focus on anything. I'd sit in front of my computer paralyzed, knowing I needed to work but feeling completely overwhelmed. My attendance basically disappeared during that period—3.6% tells that story pretty clearly.
Q13: The workload was crushing with 16 courses running simultaneously. Fatigue became my constant companion, and there were days I'd log into the LMS, stare at the dashboard, and log out without doing anything because I couldn't decide where to start. Confusion about priorities made everything worse. I'd sometimes spend more time worrying about what to do than actually doing it.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have readings open while my mind was somewhere else entirely, scrolling through pages without absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd watch videos at 2x speed just to technically complete them, knowing I'd retained almost nothing. The engagement was performative—checking boxes rather than actually learning.
Q15: I postponed the hardest assignments constantly, even knowing they were worth the most points. The anxiety made it feel impossible to start—like if I didn't try, I couldn't fail. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt better, but tomorrow I'd feel just as overwhelmed. This avoidance cost me on my assignment scores, which I know weren't great.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. They were the only thing that could cut through the paralysis and force me to act. I'd have massive productivity spikes in the 24-48 hours before something was due. My LMS activity probably looked like a heartbeat monitor—flat lines punctuated by frantic spikes. It wasn't healthy, but it was how I survived.
Q17: Feedback was actually really motivating when I got it. Positive comments made me want to engage more with that course, while constructive criticism helped me focus my limited energy. The problem was feedback often came too late to change my approach for the next assignment. I wish there had been more real-time guidance.
Q18: The course structure was honestly overwhelming. Having so many modules and requirements across 16 courses meant I was constantly context-switching. Courses with clear, predictable structures were easier to manage than ones where expectations shifted. I appreciated when everything was laid out upfront so I could at least attempt to plan, even if those plans fell apart.
Q19: Courses that chunked material into smaller, manageable pieces were much easier to engage with. When a single module had hours of content, I'd shut down before starting. Clear deadlines helped, but having too many deadlines across courses created impossible conflicts. Discussion requirements actually helped force engagement when I might have otherwise withdrawn.
Q20: I think that's about anxiety and uncertainty. You're clicking around trying to figure out what you need to do, reading instructions multiple times, checking if you understood correctly. High activity doesn't mean productive activity—sometimes it means you're lost or scared. I definitely had sessions where I was very active but accomplished nothing tangible.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. Some of my best exam preparation happened completely offline, so my performance didn't always match my LMS presence. If someone has good resources outside the system or learns effectively through methods the LMS can't track, they might do fine academically while appearing disengaged online. Efficiency matters more than visibility.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, how long you spend, what you click on. But they can't show comprehension, emotional state, or effort. They miss all the offline work, the mental processing that happens away from screens, and the difference between productive engagement and anxious clicking. They're a shadow of the real picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious, asking questions, making connections between concepts. For me, real engagement happened in quiet moments with my notes, not necessarily in the LMS. It's a mental state more than a measurable behavior.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't capture the CBT sessions that helped me manage my anxiety enough to keep going, or the hours I spent with physical notes and textbooks. It can't show that my 'happy' mood now is hard-won, or that passing this course while managing severe anxiety across 16 subjects was actually a significant achievement for me.
